front: What is the formal definition of the interior of a set A in a metric space?
back: The interior of A, denoted A° or int(A), is the union of all open sets contained in A. Equivalently, a point x is an interior point of A if there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x; ε) is entirely contained in A.

front: What is the formal definition of the closure of a set A in a metric space?
back: The closure of A, denoted Ā or cl(A), is the intersection of all closed sets containing A. Equivalently, a point x is in the closure of A if every open ball centered at x, B(x; ε) for any ε > 0, intersects A (i.e., B(x; ε) ∩ A ≠ ∅).

front: How is a limit point (or accumulation point) of a set A defined in a metric space?
back: A point x is a limit point of A if every open ball B(x; ε) contains a point of A different from x itself. The closure of A is equal to the union of A and its set of all limit points.

front: What is the relationship between the interior, closure, and boundary of a set?
back: The boundary of a set A, denoted ∂A, is defined as the closure of A minus its interior (∂A = cl(A) \ int(A)). It is the set of points where every neighborhood intersects both A and its complement.

front: True or False: The closure of an open ball B(x; r) is always the closed ball { y : d(x, y) ≤ r }.
back: False. In a general metric space, the closure of an open ball B(x; r) is contained in the closed ball, but they are not always equal. A counterexample can be constructed with a discrete metric, where the closure of B(x; 1) is just the single point {x} itself, not the entire space.
